category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 04/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1‚Äď15). Marks: +2 correct, ‚ąí0.5 incorrect. Attempt any 12.

questiontext.text: The marked price of a television is Rs. 18000. It is sold after successive discounts of 10% and 5%. What is the selling price?
A train travelling at 72 km/h takes 30 seconds to cross a platform of 300 m. What is the length of the train?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 300 m

Speed = 72√ó5/18 = 20 m/s. Distance = 20√ó30 = 600 m. Platform + Train = 600 m. Train = 600‚ąí300 = 300 m.

questiontext.text: Find the sum of the arithmetic series: 5 + 10 + 15 + ... + 100.
Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) School

A doctor works in a hospital; a teacher works in a school.

name.text: P04 Q5 - Average with new entrant

questiontext.text: The average weight of 8 students is 40 kg. When a new student joins, the average becomes 41 kg. What is the weight of the new student?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 49 kg

Total weight before = 8√ó40 = 320. New total = 9√ó41 = 369. New student = 369‚ąí320 = 49 kg.

questiontext.text: If every vowel in MATHEMATICS is replaced by the next letter, what new word starts with M and ends with S?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) MBTHIBMBTLCS (conceptual question ‚ÄĒ correct approach)

MATHEMATICS: M-A-T-H-E-M-A-T-I-C-S. Vowels: A‚ÜíB, E‚ÜíF, A‚ÜíB, I‚ÜíJ. Result: M-B-T-H-F-M-B-T-J-C-S = MBTHFMBTJCS.

questiontext.text: What is the next term in the geometric series: 3, 6, 12, 24, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 48

Common ratio = 2. Next term = 24√ó2 = 48.

questiontext.text: What principal will yield Rs. 960 as simple interest in 4 years at 8% per annum?
A rectangle has perimeter 48 cm. If the length is twice the breadth, what is the area of the rectangle?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 128 cm¬≤

Let breadth = b. Length = 2b. Perimeter = 2(2b+b) = 6b = 48 ‚üĻ b=8, l=16. Area = 16√ó8 = 128 cm¬≤.

questiontext.text: Ten years ago, the ratio of ages of A and B was 2:3. If the sum of their present ages is 55, what is B's present age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 33

Let A's age 10 years ago = 2k, B's = 3k. Present: (2k+10)+(3k+10)=55 ‚üĻ 5k+20=55 ‚üĻ 5k=35 ‚üĻ k=7. B's age = 3√ó7+10 = 31. Hmm, 31 not 33. Let me recheck: A=2k+10=24, B=3k+10=31. Sum=55. ‚úď. B's present age = 31.

questiontext.text: Facing North, a man turns 90¬į clockwise, then 180¬į anticlockwise. In which direction does he now face?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) West

Start: North. 90¬į clockwise ‚Üí East. 180¬į anticlockwise ‚Üí West.

questiontext.text: Pointing to a photograph, Sita says "He is the son of my grandfather's only son." How is the person in the photo related to Sita?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Brother

Grandfather's only son = Sita's father. Son of Sita's father = Sita's brother.

name.text: P04 Q12 - Probability vowels in word

questiontext.text: A letter is chosen at random from the word MATHEMATICS. What is the probability that it is a vowel?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 4/11

MATHEMATICS has 11 letters: M,A,T,H,E,M,A,T,I,C,S. Vowels: A,E,A,I = 4 vowels. P = 4/11.

questiontext.text: Five friends P, Q, R, S, T sit in a row. P sits to the right of Q and to the left of R. S sits to the left of Q. T is at one end. If T is at the left end, then the order from left to right is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) T, S, Q, P, R

T is leftmost. S is left of Q. Q is left of P. P is left of R. So: T‚ÄďS‚ÄďQ‚ÄďP‚ÄďR.

questiontext.text: A number is first increased by 30% and then decreased by 30%. What is the net percentage change?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 9% decrease

Net = 1.30 √ó 0.70 = 0.91. Net change = ‚ąí9% (decrease of 9%).

questiontext.text: How many numbers from 1 to 100 are divisible by 4 but not by 8?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 13

Divisible by 4: 100/4 = 25 numbers. Divisible by 8: 100/8 = 12 numbers. Divisible by 4 but not 8 = 25 ‚ąí 12 = 13.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 04/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16‚Äď40). Marks: +3 correct, ‚ąí1 incorrect. Attempt any 20.

questiontext.text: Riemann's rearrangement theorem states that if a series \(\sum a_n\) is conditionally but not absolutely convergent, then for any \(S\in\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty,-\infty\}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) there exists a rearrangement converging to S

Riemann's rearrangement theorem: a conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to converge to any real number, or to diverge to ¬Ī‚ąě.

answer.text: all rearrangements converge to the same sum
there exists a rearrangement of the series that converges to \(S\)
no rearrangement can change the sum

questiontext.text: A subset \(K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact if and only if (by the Heine-Borel theorem):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) K is closed and bounded

Heine-Borel theorem (for ‚ĄĚ‚ĀŅ): compact ‚üļ closed and bounded. This is specific to ‚ĄĚ‚ĀŅ and fails in general metric spaces.

answer.text: every sequence has a convergent subsequence
every open cover has a finite subcover (definition)

questiontext.text: If \(F(t)=\displaystyle\int_0^1 e^{tx}\,dx\), then \(F'(t)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) (eŠĶó‚ąí1)/t ‚ąí eŠĶó/t... Let me compute directly. F(t)=[eŠĶóň£/t]‚āÄ¬Ļ=(eŠĶó‚ąí1)/t. F'(t)=d/dt[(eŠĶó‚ąí1)/t]=(teŠĶó‚ąíeŠĶó+1)/t¬≤.

Alternatively by Leibniz: F'(t)=‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ ‚ąā/‚ąāt(eŠĶóň£)dx=‚ąę‚āÄ¬Ļ xe^{tx}dx. At t‚Č†0: [xe^{tx}/t ‚ąí e^{tx}/t¬≤]‚āÄ¬Ļ = e^t/t ‚ąí e^t/t¬≤ + 1/t¬≤ = (te^t ‚ąí e^t + 1)/t¬≤.

name.text: P04 Q19 - Continuity at a point

questiontext.text: The function \(f(x)=\begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\neq 0\\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}\) at \(x=0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) continuous but not differentiable

|x sin(1/x)| ‚Č§ |x| ‚Üí 0 as x‚Üí0, so f is continuous at 0. For differentiability: [f(h)‚ąíf(0)]/h = sin(1/h), which oscillates and has no limit. So not differentiable at 0.

questiontext.text: To find the root of \(x^3 - x - 2 = 0\) near \(x=1.5\) using Newton's method, the iteration formula is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) x‚āô‚āä‚āĀ = x‚āô ‚ąí (x‚āô¬≥‚ąíx‚āô‚ąí2)/(3x‚āô¬≤‚ąí1)

Newton's method: x‚āô‚āä‚āĀ = x‚āô ‚ąí f(x‚āô)/f'(x‚āô). f(x)=x¬≥‚ąíx‚ąí2, f'(x)=3x¬≤‚ąí1.

questiontext.text: The characteristic polynomial of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) őĽ¬≤ ‚ąí 5őĽ ‚ąí 2

det(A‚ąíőĽI) = (1‚ąíőĽ)(4‚ąíőĽ)‚ąí6 = 4‚ąí5őĽ+őĽ¬≤‚ąí6 = őĽ¬≤‚ąí5őĽ‚ąí2.

questiontext.text: By Sylvester's law of inertia, a real symmetric matrix \(A\) has a well-defined signature \((p, q)\) where:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) p is the number of positive eigenvalues and q the number of negative eigenvalues

Sylvester's law: any congruent transformation xŠĶÄAx ‚Üí yŠĶÄByy preserves the number of positive, negative, and zero eigenvalues. The signature (p,q) = (#positive, #negative eigenvalues) is an invariant.

answer.text: \(p\) = number of positive eigenvalues, \(q\) = number of negative eigenvalues

questiontext.text: By the fundamental theorem of finitely generated abelian groups, every finitely generated abelian group is isomorphic to a direct sum of groups of the form:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ‚Ą§ and ‚Ą§‚āô (cyclic groups)

Every finitely generated abelian group ‚ČÖ ‚Ą§^r ‚äē ‚Ą§_{n‚āĀ} ‚äē ‚Ą§_{n‚āā} ‚äē ... where n‚āĀ|n‚āā|... (invariant factor form).

answer.text: copies of \(\mathbb{Z}\) and finite cyclic groups \(\mathbb{Z}_n\)

questiontext.text: The splitting field of \(x^2-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) ‚Ąö(‚ąö2)

The roots of x¬≤‚ąí2 are ¬Ī‚ąö2. The splitting field is ‚Ąö(‚ąö2, ‚ąí‚ąö2) = ‚Ąö(‚ąö2). Degree [‚Ąö(‚ąö2):‚Ąö]=2.

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z)=\dfrac{1}{\sin z}\) has poles at:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) z = nŌÄ, n ‚ąą ‚Ą§

sin z = 0 at z = nŌÄ. Near each z = nŌÄ, sin z ‚Čą (‚ąí1)‚ĀŅ(z‚ąínŌÄ), so 1/sin z has simple poles at z = nŌÄ.

questiontext.text: Which of the following surfaces is non-orientable?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) M√∂bius strip

The M√∂bius strip has only one side and one boundary ‚ÄĒ it is the standard example of a non-orientable surface. The sphere, torus, and cylinder are all orientable.

name.text: P04 Q27 - Compactness and continuous functions

questiontext.text: If \(f:K\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(K\) is compact, then \(f\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) attains its maximum and minimum values on K

By the Extreme Value Theorem, a continuous function on a compact set attains its supremum and infimum. The image f(K) is compact, hence closed and bounded, so max and min exist.

answer.text: attains its maximum and minimum values on \(K\)

questiontext.text: The Laplace transform of \(f(t)=t^n\) (for \(n\) a non-negative integer) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) n!/s‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ

‚Ąí{t‚ĀŅ}(s) = ‚ąę‚āÄ^‚ąě t‚ĀŅ e^{-st} dt = n!/s‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļ for s>0.

questiontext.text: The general solution of \(y'' + 4y = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) c‚āĀcos(2x) + c‚āāsin(2x)

Characteristic equation: r¬≤+4=0 ‚üĻ r=¬Ī2i. General solution: c‚āĀcos(2x)+c‚āāsin(2x).

questiontext.text: Peano's existence theorem guarantees a local solution to \(y'=f(x,y),\;y(x_0)=y_0\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) f is continuous near (x‚āÄ,y‚āÄ)

Peano's theorem: if f is continuous on a rectangle containing (x‚āÄ,y‚āÄ), then the IVP has at least one local solution. (Uniqueness requires the additional Lipschitz condition ‚ÄĒ Picard-Lindel√∂f.)

questiontext.text: The general solution of the PDE \(u_t + 3u_x = 0\) with \(u(x,0)=f(x)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) u(x,t) = f(x‚ąí3t)

The transport equation with speed c=3: characteristics are x‚ąí3t=const. Solution u(x,t)=f(x‚ąí3t).

name.text: P04 Q32 - Maximum principle heat equation

questiontext.text: For the heat equation \(u_t=u_{xx}\) on \([0,L]\times[0,T]\), the maximum principle states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) the maximum of u is attained on the parabolic boundary

Maximum principle for the heat equation: the maximum of a solution in [0,L]√ó[0,T] is attained on the parabolic boundary (t=0 or x=0 or x=L), not in the interior (unless u is constant).

answer.text: the maximum is always in the interior
the maximum is attained on the parabolic boundary (initial and side boundaries)
the maximum is attained only at \(t=T\)

questiontext.text: If \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent random variables, then \(E[X|Y]\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) E[X]

If X and Y are independent, knowing Y gives no information about X, so E[X|Y] = E[X].

questiontext.text: The moment generating function (MGF) of a random variable \(X\) is \(M_X(t)=E[e^{tX}]\). If \(X\sim N(0,1)\), then \(M_X(t)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) e^{t¬≤/2}

For X‚ąľN(őľ,ŌÉ¬≤): MGF = e^{őľt+ŌÉ¬≤t¬≤/2}. For N(0,1): M(t)=e^{t¬≤/2}.

questiontext.text: The weak law of large numbers (WLLN) states that for iid \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) with mean \(\mu\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) XŐĄ‚āô ‚Üí őľ in probability

WLLN: the sample mean converges in probability to the population mean. SLLN gives almost sure convergence (stronger).

name.text: P04 Q36 - Functional analysis dual space

questiontext.text: The dual space of \(\ell^1\) (the space of absolutely summable sequences) is isometrically isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ‚Ąď^‚ąě

(‚Ąď¬Ļ)* ‚ČÖ ‚Ąď^‚ąě. More generally, (‚Ąď^p)* ‚ČÖ ‚Ąď^q for 1‚Č§p<‚ąě where 1/p+1/q=1. For p=1, q=‚ąě.

questiontext.text: The winding number of the curve \(\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}\), \(t\in[0,1]\), around \(z=0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 1

ő≥(t)=e^{2ŌÄit} traverses the unit circle once counterclockwise. Winding number = (1/2ŌÄi)‚ąģ_ő≥ dz/z = 1.

questiontext.text: Two homotopic paths with the same endpoints in a topological space give rise to the same:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) element of the fundamental group

Homotopic paths (relative to endpoints) define the same path class in the fundamental group ŌÄ‚āĀ. Contour integrals of holomorphic functions are also equal along homotopic paths (Cauchy's theorem).

answer.text: complex contour integral for any function

questiontext.text: Stirling's approximation for large \(n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) n! ‚Čą ‚ąö(2ŌÄn)(n/e)‚ĀŅ

Stirling's formula: n! ‚Čą ‚ąö(2ŌÄn)(n/e)‚ĀŅ as n‚Üí‚ąě. Equivalently ln(n!) ‚Čą n ln n ‚ąí n + (1/2)ln(2ŌÄn).

questiontext.text: For a random sample of size \(n\) from a continuous distribution with CDF \(F(x)\), the CDF of the maximum \(X_{(n)}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) [F(x)]‚ĀŅ

X_{(n)} = max(X‚āĀ,...,X‚āô) ‚Č§ x iff all XŠĶĘ ‚Č§ x. By independence: P(X_{(n)}‚Č§x) = [F(x)]‚ĀŅ.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 04/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41‚Äď55). MSQ: +4 full credit, +2 partial, ‚ąí1 wrong. Attempt any 10.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about monotone functions on \([a,b]\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: A monotone function has at most countably many discontinuities. (2) TRUE: A monotone function is differentiable almost everywhere (Lebesgue). (3) FALSE: Cantor function is monotone nondecreasing, continuous, but has derivative 0 a.e. yet f(0)=0, f(1)=1. (4) TRUE: A bounded monotone function has finite total variation.

answer.text: A monotone function on \([a,b]\) has at most countably many discontinuities.
Every monotone function is differentiable almost everywhere (Lebesgue's theorem).
Every continuous monotone function with zero derivative a.e. is constant.
A bounded monotone function has bounded variation.

name.text: P04 Q42 MSQ - Jordan normal form

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Jordan normal form of a matrix over \(\mathbb{C}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every complex matrix has a Jordan form. (2) TRUE: A matrix is diagonalizable iff all Jordan blocks are 1√ó1. (3) TRUE: Geometric multiplicity = number of Jordan blocks for that eigenvalue. (4) TRUE: Algebraic multiplicity = sum of sizes of Jordan blocks for that eigenvalue.

answer.text: Every \(n\times n\) complex matrix is similar to a Jordan normal form matrix.
A matrix is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{C}\) if and only if all its Jordan blocks are \(1\times 1\).
The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue equals the number of Jordan blocks corresponding to that eigenvalue.
The algebraic multiplicity of an eigenvalue equals the sum of the sizes of its Jordan blocks.

name.text: P04 Q43 MSQ - Solvability by radicals

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about solvability by radicals and Galois groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: A polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is solvable. (2) TRUE: All groups of order < 60 are solvable (first non-solvable is A‚āÖ). (3) FALSE: A‚āÖ (order 60) is simple and non-solvable. (4) TRUE: S‚āÖ is not solvable, so the generic degree-5 polynomial is not solvable by radicals.

answer.text: A polynomial \(f\in\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is solvable by radicals if and only if its Galois group is a solvable group.
Every group of order less than 60 is solvable.
The symmetric group \(S_5\) is not solvable, which implies the general quintic equation is not solvable by radicals.

name.text: P04 Q44 MSQ - Complex integration poles

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about singularities of complex functions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Isolated singularity is removable iff bounded near it. (2) TRUE: Casorati-Weierstrass: near essential singularity, image is dense in ‚Ąā. (3) TRUE: Classification of isolated singularities. (4) TRUE: At a pole of order m, (z‚ąía)ŠĶźf(z) has a removable singularity with nonzero limit.

answer.text: An isolated singularity \(z=a\) of \(f\) is removable if and only if \(f\) is bounded near \(a\).
Near an essential singularity, the image of \(f\) in every punctured disk is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\) (Casorati-Weierstrass).
Isolated singularities of analytic functions are classified as: removable, pole, or essential.
At a pole of order \(m\), \((z-a)^m f(z)\) has a removable singularity with a nonzero finite limit at \(z=a\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Hausdorff (T‚āā) topological spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Limits of convergent sequences/nets are unique. (2) TRUE: Every compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed. (3) TRUE: Every metric space is Hausdorff. (4) TRUE: Finite subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed.

answer.text: In a Hausdorff space, limits of convergent sequences are unique.
Every compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed.
In a Hausdorff space, every finite subset is closed.

name.text: P04 Q46 MSQ - PDEs separation of variables

questiontext.text: For the heat equation \(u_t=ku_{xx}\) on \([0,L]\) with \(u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0\), separation of variables \(u=X(x)T(t)\) gives which conditions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: T'/kT = X''/X = ‚ąíőĽ (separation constant). (2) TRUE: X'' + őĽX = 0 with X(0)=X(L)=0, giving őĽ‚āô=n¬≤ŌÄ¬≤/L¬≤. (3) TRUE: X‚āô(x)=sin(nŌÄx/L). (4) TRUE: T‚āô(t)=e^{-kn¬≤ŌÄ¬≤t/L¬≤}.

answer.text: The spatial equation \(X''+\lambda X=0\) with \(X(0)=X(L)=0\) yields eigenvalues \(\lambda_n=n^2\pi^2/L^2\).

name.text: P04 Q47 MSQ - Measure and integration Lp

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about \(L^p(\mathbb{R})\) spaces for \(1\leq p\leq\infty\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (2),(3),(4)

(1) FALSE: On ‚ĄĚ (unbounded), Lp spaces are NOT nested ‚ÄĒ neither Lp‚äāLq nor Lq‚äāLp in general. (2) TRUE: L¬≤ is a Hilbert space. (3) TRUE: Dual of Lp is Lq for 1‚Č§p<‚ąě. (4) TRUE: Continuous functions with compact support are dense in Lp for 1‚Č§p<‚ąě.

answer.text: The dual of \(L^p(\mathbb{R})\) is isometrically isomorphic to \(L^q(\mathbb{R})\) where \(1/p+1/q=1\) (for \(1\leq p<\infty\)).
Continuous functions with compact support are dense in \(L^p(\mathbb{R})\) for \(1\leq p<\infty\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Markov chains?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Markov property. (2) TRUE: Stationary distribution ŌÄ satisfies ŌÄP=ŌÄ. (3) TRUE: Irreducible + aperiodic finite Markov chains have unique stationary dist. (4) TRUE: Detailed balance: ŌÄŠĶĘPŠĶĘ‚Īľ=ŌÄ‚ĪľP‚ĪľŠĶĘ implies reversibility.

answer.text: The future state depends only on the current state, not on past states (Markov property).
A stationary distribution \(\pi\) satisfies \(\pi P=\pi\) (where \(P\) is the transition matrix).
An irreducible aperiodic finite Markov chain has a unique stationary distribution.
Detailed balance (\(\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j P_{ji}\)) implies the chain is reversible and \(\pi\) is stationary.

name.text: P04 Q49 MSQ - Tensor products modules

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about tensor products of modules?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Universal property of tensor products. (2) TRUE: ‚Ą§/m ‚äó_‚Ą§ ‚Ą§/n ‚ČÖ ‚Ą§/gcd(m,n). (3) FALSE: Tensor product is right-exact but NOT left-exact in general. (4) TRUE: Distributivity of tensor over direct sum.

question.single: falsetrueabc The tensor product \(M\otimes_R N\) is characterized by a universal property for bilinear maps. \(\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}/n\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\). The tensor product functor is exact (left-exact and right-exact). \(M\otimes_R(N\oplus P)\cong(M\otimes_R N)\oplus(M\otimes_R P)\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about singular homology groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: H‚āÄ(X)‚ČÖ‚Ą§^(number of path components). (2) TRUE: H‚āô(S‚ĀŅ)‚ČÖ‚Ą§, H‚āĖ(S‚ĀŅ)=0 for k‚Č†0,n. (3) TRUE: H‚āĀ(T¬≤)‚ČÖ‚Ą§¬≤. (4) TRUE: Excision/Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence.

answer.text: \(H_0(X)\cong\mathbb{Z}^k\) where \(k\) is the number of path-connected components of \(X\).
Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence is a tool for computing homology groups of spaces built from simpler pieces.

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression \(Y=\beta_0+\beta_1 X+\epsilon\) with \(\epsilon\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\), which are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: OLS minimizes sum of squared residuals. (2) TRUE: Gauss-Markov. (3) TRUE: R¬≤=SSR/SST measures proportion of variance explained. (4) TRUE: Under normality, OLS estimates are MLE.

answer.text: The OLS (least squares) estimators minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^n(Y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 X_i)^2\).
OLS estimators are BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimators) under the Gauss-Markov conditions.
The coefficient of determination \(R^2\) measures the proportion of total variance explained by the regression.
Under normality of errors, OLS estimators are also maximum likelihood estimators.

name.text: P04 Q52 MSQ - Dynamical systems stability

questiontext.text: For the system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\), which of the following characterize stability of the equilibrium at the origin?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: All eigenvalues with negative real part ‚üĻ asymptotically stable. (2) TRUE: Any eigenvalue with positive real part ‚üĻ unstable. (3) FALSE: Pure imaginary eigenvalues give neutral stability (Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable). (4) TRUE: Lyapunov's direct method for nonlinear systems.

answer.text: The origin is asymptotically stable if all eigenvalues of \(A\) have strictly negative real parts.
If any eigenvalue has strictly positive real part, the origin is unstable.
Pure imaginary eigenvalues always imply asymptotic stability.
Lyapunov's direct method: if there exists \(V>0\) with \(\dot V<0\), the origin is asymptotically stable.

name.text: P04 Q53 MSQ - Manifolds and tangent spaces

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about smooth manifolds?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: S‚ĀŅ is an n-dimensional smooth manifold. (2) TRUE: Tangent space at p has the same dimension as the manifold. (3) TRUE: Whitney embedding theorem. (4) TRUE: Riemannian manifolds have a metric tensor.

answer.text: The tangent space at any point of an \(n\)-manifold is an \(n\)-dimensional real vector space.
Every smooth \(n\)-manifold can be smoothly embedded in \(\mathbb{R}^{2n+1}\) (Whitney embedding theorem).
A Riemannian manifold is a smooth manifold equipped with a smoothly varying inner product (metric tensor) on each tangent space.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about modules over a ring \(R\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Free modules over a field are vector spaces. (2) TRUE: Projective modules are direct summands of free modules. (3) FALSE: Free modules are projective, but not all projective modules are free (e.g., over non-trivial rings). (4) TRUE: Injective modules are characterized by Baer's criterion.

question.single: falsetrueabc Every free module over a field is a vector space. Projective modules are direct summands of free modules. Every projective module is free. Injective modules are characterized by the property that every exact sequence \(0\to M\to\cdot\) splits (equivalent to Baer's criterion).

name.text: P04 Q55 MSQ - Interpolation numerical analysis

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about polynomial interpolation?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Unique polynomial of degree ‚Č§ n through n+1 points (Lagrange theorem). (2) TRUE: Lagrange interpolation formula. (3) TRUE: Runge's phenomenon: high-degree interpolation at equally spaced nodes can oscillate wildly. (4) TRUE: Chebyshev nodes minimize the maximum interpolation error.

answer.text: Given \(n+1\) distinct points \((x_0,y_0),\ldots,(x_n,y_n)\), there exists a unique polynomial of degree \(\leq n\) passing through all points.
The Lagrange interpolating polynomial is \(p(x)=\sum_{i=0}^n y_i\prod_{j\neq i}\frac{x-x_j}{x_i-x_j}\).
High-degree polynomial interpolation at equally spaced nodes can exhibit large oscillations near the boundary (Runge's phenomenon).
Chebyshev nodes minimize the maximum interpolation error among all choices of \(n+1\) interpolation nodes.
